comments:
Summary
This paper proposes a semi-supervised convolutional neural network architecture for facial gesture recognition in video. The main technical novelty is a "multi-velocity" layer that performs temporal resampling by initializing a convolutional layer with coefficients derived from cubic B-spline interpolation to produce intermediate frames (different effective speeds). Three such velocity-pathways are trained in parallel (concatenated features) and combined with an autoencoder+classification loss schedule to leverage a very large unlabeled video collection alongside a smaller labeled set. The authors also report collecting and (intend to) release a very large face-video dataset (≈162M images in 6.5M clips) plus a labeled Asevo dataset (2777 clips). Experiments on CK, MMI and Asevo show improved accuracy vs a collection of prior methods and baseline deep nets.

Strengths
- The multi-velocity idea (explicitly handling different action speeds via temporal resampling) is interesting and addresses a real issue in video-based gesture/action recognition. Encoding B-spline interpolation as an initial convolutional weight matrix is clever and practical.
- A pragmatic semi-supervised training protocol is described (autoencoder pretraining then joint fine-tuning with a weighted classification loss), which is relevant when labeled video data are scarce.
- The authors assembled a very large unlabeled face-video collection and a modest labeled dataset; such resources (if released) are valuable to the community.
- Reported results indicate strong performance on standard benchmarks (CK, MMI) and on the authors' dataset.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Experimental details are insufficient. Key elements missing or under-specified: exact training procedures and hyperparameters (learning rates, schedules, batch sizes), hardware/training time, how the baseline networks were trained or fine-tuned for fair comparison, precise evaluation protocols (e.g. subject-independent splits, whether standard protocols for CK+/MMI were followed), class balancing, and exact metrics reported (confusion matrices are mentioned but not fully presented here).
- Comparisons to prior work appear incomplete or possibly not apples-to-apples. The paper states state-of-the-art numbers but does not clearly state whether identical splits and preprocessing were used. It is unclear whether competing deep baselines ([25], GoogleNet) were trained end-to-end on the same data or were off-the-shelf.
- Ablation studies are lacking. The contribution of (i) the multi-velocity layer vs a single pathway, (ii) initialized spline weights vs randomly initialized learnable temporal filters, (iii) the semi-supervised pretraining schedule and the loss weighting scheme are not quantified. These ablations are crucial to support the central claims.
- The mathematical description of the spline-to-convolution mapping is plausible but terse and hard to follow in places; it is not fully clear whether the RA^{-1}T weights are fixed or fine-tuned during training and how numerical stability/scaling is handled.
- Dataset issues: The large autoencoder dataset is described as obtained from news sources (CNN, MSNBC, FOX, CSPAN) and filtered using Viola-Jones + pose heuristics. This likely introduces biases (demographic, frontal pose, studio lighting) and the reliance on Viola-Jones restricts to near-frontal faces. The paper acknowledges frontal-only limitation but should discuss dataset bias and privacy/consent/ethical considerations for public release.
- Presentation quality: the manuscript has repetitions and some formatting/typo issues, which reduce clarity. Several statements are duplicated in the provided text and some algorithmic pseudocode is not fully self-explanatory.

Suggestions for improvement
1) Provide full experimental details: training hyperparameters, data splits (subject IDs used in train/test/val or cross-validation protocol), augmentation details, batch sizes, number of training iterations/epochs, and hardware used. Clarify how competing methods and baselines were trained or obtained.
2) Add ablation experiments isolating the multi-velocity layer’s contribution (single-path baseline, different numbers of velocities, learned vs fixed spline weights), and the effect of the semi-supervised pretraining and loss-weight schedule.
3) Show and discuss the learned interpolation (sampling) factors after training and/or visualize the filters to demonstrate that the velocity layer adapts as claimed.
4) Provide more comprehensive evaluation tables and full confusion matrices for public datasets and report statistical significance where possible. Make clear whether standard CK+/MMI evaluation protocols are followed.
5) Discuss dataset bias, collection methodology, licensing and privacy/consent issues regarding the planned public release, and provide more metadata about the 6.5M clips (source breakdown, face pose distributions, demographic information if available).
6) Improve exposition: remove duplicated paragraphs, clean up algorithm pseudocode, and clarify the spline derivation and how it is integrated into the network (fixed vs learnable).

Soundness remarks
The overall approach is plausible and the spline derivation appears mathematically reasonable. However, because important experimental and implementation details are omitted from the provided text, it is difficult to fully assess reproducibility and the soundness of the empirical claims. In particular, how spline-initialized layers are trained (frozen vs fine-tuned), numerical conditioning of RA^{-1}T for long clips, and the computational cost of the multi-path architecture on large-scale data are not addressed.

Recommendation rationale
I find the paper to be of good quality with an interesting, potentially impactful idea (velocity-invariant layers) and a strong engineering effort (very large dataset). However, the manuscript as provided lacks sufficient experimental detail and ablation evidence to fully support its central claims. If these gaps are addressed (clarify training/evaluation, add ablations and interpretability/visualization of the velocity module, and discuss dataset biases/ethics), the work would be a solid contribution. Given the current state of the write-up I recommend a positive but not maximal score and encourage revisions that address the points above.
